(also known as **jazz hop** or **jazz hip-hop**) is a fusion of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and [hip hop music](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop"), as well as an [alternative hip-hop](/wiki/Alternative_hip-hop "Alternative hip-hop") subgenre,[[1]](#cite_note-AM-1) that developed in the late 1980s and early 1990s. An article on [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") explains that the genre "was an attempt to fuse [African-American music](/wiki/African-American_music "African-American music") of the past with a newly dominant form of the present, paying tribute to and reinvigorating the former while expanding the horizons of the latter." The rhythm was rooted in hip hop[[1]](#cite_note-AM-1) over which repetitive phrases of jazz instrumentation, such as the trumpet, double bass, etc., were placed. The groups involved in the formation of jazz rap included [A Tribe Called Quest](/wiki/A_Tribe_Called_Quest "A Tribe Called Quest"), [Digable Planets](/wiki/Digable_Planets "Digable Planets"), [De La Soul](/wiki/De_La_Soul "De La Soul"), [Gang Starr](/wiki/Gang_Starr "Gang Starr"), and [Jungle Brothers](/wiki/Jungle_Brothers "Jungle Brothers").[[1]](#cite_note-AM-1)

**Jazz rap** (also known as **jazz hop** or **jazz hip-hop**) is a fusion of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and [hip hop music](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop"), as well as an [alternative hip-hop](/wiki/Alternative_hip-hop "Alternative hip-hop") subgenre,[[1]](#cite_note-AM-1) that developed in the late 1980s and early 1990s. An article on [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") explains that the genre "was an attempt to fuse [African-American music](/wiki/African-American_music "African-American music") of the past with a newly dominant form of the present, paying tribute to and reinvigorating the former while expanding the horizons of the latter." The rhythm was rooted in hip hop[[1]](#cite_note-AM-1) over which repetitive phrases of jazz instrumentation, such as the trumpet, double bass, etc., were placed. The groups involved in the formation of jazz rap included [A Tribe Called Quest](/wiki/A_Tribe_Called_Quest "A Tribe Called Quest"), [Digable Planets](/wiki/Digable_Planets "Digable Planets"), [De La Soul](/wiki/De_La_Soul "De La Soul"), [Gang Starr](/wiki/Gang_Starr "Gang Starr"), and [Jungle Brothers](/wiki/Jungle_Brothers "Jungle Brothers").[[1]](#cite_note-AM-1)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_rap&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The London club scene in the 1960s and 1970s became a canvas for the rare mixing of Jazz tracks. Such tracks were from the Blue Note catalogue, a collection of American Jazz music. Accompanied by percussion rhythms, a splash of funk, producing a new sub-genre known as acid jazz. Stepping into the 1980s, DJs innovated this sound further while implementing techniques. Collaborations between hip-hop DJs and jazz musicians such as Herbie Hancock and DJ Grand Mixer D.ST with their 1983 song "Rockit" laid a crucial foundation for what would soon become jazz rap.

[At this time](/wiki/1980s_in_jazz "1980s in jazz"), the jazz community was divided between those who appreciated traditional styles and those who embraced newer forms like smooth jazz. This period also marked a significant shift in jazz's cultural positioning, elevating it to the status of "serious art music." Influential figures like [Wynton Marsalis](/wiki/Wynton_Marsalis "Wynton Marsalis") played a pivotal role in this transformation, advocating for a return to traditional jazz values.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Jazz rap's emergence can be seen as an attempt to elevate rap music's status by associating it with jazz's [cultural capital](/wiki/Cultural_capital "Cultural capital") , and was seen as an alternative to dominant rap subgenres like [gangsta](/wiki/Gangsta_rap "Gangsta rap") and [pop rap](/wiki/Pop_rap "Pop rap"). This association not only enriched the musical texture of hip-hop but also provided a platform for [social](/wiki/Social_commentary "Social commentary") and [political commentary](/wiki/Political_commentary "Political commentary"), aligning with jazz's historical role as a voice for [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") experiences and struggles.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_rap&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

In 1989, [Gang Starr](/wiki/Gang_Starr "Gang Starr") released the debut single "Words I Manifest", sampling [Dizzy Gillespie](/wiki/Dizzy_Gillespie "Dizzy Gillespie")'s 1952 "Night in Tunisia", and [Stetsasonic](/wiki/Stetsasonic "Stetsasonic") released "Talkin' All That Jazz", sampling [Lonnie Liston Smith](/wiki/Lonnie_Liston_Smith "Lonnie Liston Smith"). Gang Starr's debut LP, *[No More Mr. Nice Guy](/wiki/No_More_Mr._Nice_Guy_(Gang_Starr_album) "No More Mr. Nice Guy (Gang Starr album)")* ([Wild Pitch](/wiki/Wild_Pitch_Records "Wild Pitch Records"), 1989), and their track "Jazz Thing" ([CBS](/wiki/Columbia_Records#The_1960s "Columbia Records"), 1990) for the soundtrack of *[Mo' Better Blues](/wiki/Mo%27_Better_Blues "Mo' Better Blues")*, further popularized the jazz rap style. In 1992, [Eric B & Rakim](/wiki/Eric_B._%26_Rakim "Eric B. & Rakim") used wood bass on "[Don't Sweat the Technique](/wiki/Don%27t_Sweat_the_Technique_(song) "Don't Sweat the Technique (song)")".[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[Digable Planets](/wiki/Digable_Planets "Digable Planets")' 1993 release *[Reachin' (A New Refutation of Time and Space)](/wiki/Reachin%27_(A_New_Refutation_of_Time_and_Space) "Reachin' (A New Refutation of Time and Space)")* was a hit jazz rap record. It sampled the likes of [Don Cherry](/wiki/Don_Cherry_(jazz) "Don Cherry (jazz)"), [Sonny Rollins](/wiki/Sonny_Rollins "Sonny Rollins"), [Art Blakey](/wiki/Art_Blakey "Art Blakey"), [Herbie Mann](/wiki/Herbie_Mann "Herbie Mann"), [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock"), [Grant Green](/wiki/Grant_Green "Grant Green"), and [Rahsaan Roland Kirk](/wiki/Rahsaan_Roland_Kirk "Rahsaan Roland Kirk"). Additionally, it spawned the hit single "[Rebirth of Slick (Cool Like Dat)](/wiki/Rebirth_of_Slick_(Cool_Like_Dat) "Rebirth of Slick (Cool Like Dat)")".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 1993, [Us3](/wiki/Us3 "Us3") released *[Hand on the Torch](/wiki/Hand_on_the_Torch "Hand on the Torch")* on [Blue Note Records](/wiki/Blue_Note_Records "Blue Note Records"). All samples were from the Blue Note catalogue. The single "[Cantaloop](/wiki/Cantaloop "Cantaloop")" was Blue Note's first [gold](/wiki/RIAA_certification#List_of_certifications "RIAA certification") record.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Post-[WWII](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music") and modern jazz had fused with the introduction of [Black appeal radio](/wiki/Black-appeal_stations "Black-appeal stations"), which attracted a younger audience through its reliance on [jive](/wiki/African-American_Vernacular_English "African-American Vernacular English") idioms, rhyming, and cadence-laden rap verses. [Dizzy Gillespie](/wiki/Dizzy_Gillespie "Dizzy Gillespie") had pointed to *The Jives of [Dr. Hepcat](/wiki/Dr._Hepcat "Dr. Hepcat")* and rhyming D.J. [Daddy-O Daylie](/wiki/Holmes_Daylie "Holmes Daylie") as key to popularizing modern jazz.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The rise of [Top-40](/wiki/Top-40 "Top-40") radio on the strength of the rapping DJs in this period of radio's rebirth among black youth led to the wider use of language and syntax popularizing rap. [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali")'s phrasing to the press in the early part of his career was born of listening to black radio of the 1950s, which was often white radio announcers speaking slang "jive" and imitating black announcers while withholding the fact on air of their backgrounds.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Pioneering DJs [Al Benson](/wiki/Al_Benson "Al Benson"), [Nat D.](/wiki/Nathanial_Dowd_Gaston_Williams "Nathanial Dowd Gaston Williams"), and [Jack the Rapper](/wiki/Joseph_Deighton_Gibson_Jr. "Joseph Deighton Gibson Jr.") all used rhyming,[[9]](#cite_note-9) the dozens, and jive talk to pepper their broadcasts and were widely copied by white DJs like John Richbourg, Gene Nobles, and Bill Allen during the 1950s, whose influence on [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown") and other godfathers of rap was formative. [Bebop](/wiki/Bebop "Bebop") was the backing track that modern jazz credits with being the foundation of black appeal, radio is based on.[[10]](#cite_note-exhibition-10)

### Native Tongues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_rap&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Native Tongues")]

Groups making up the collective known as the [Native Tongues](/wiki/Native_Tongues "Native Tongues") tended toward jazzy releases: these include the [Jungle Brothers](/wiki/Jungle_Brothers "Jungle Brothers")' debut, *[Straight Out the Jungle](/wiki/Straight_Out_the_Jungle "Straight Out the Jungle")* (Warlock, 1988), and [A Tribe Called Quest](/wiki/A_Tribe_Called_Quest "A Tribe Called Quest")'s *[People's Instinctive Travels and the Paths of Rhythm](/wiki/People%27s_Instinctive_Travels_and_the_Paths_of_Rhythm "People's Instinctive Travels and the Paths of Rhythm")* ([Jive](/wiki/Jive_Records "Jive Records"), 1990).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] *[The Low End Theory](/wiki/The_Low_End_Theory "The Low End Theory")* has become one of hip hop's most acclaimed albums,[[11]](#cite_note-11) and also earned praise from jazz bassist [Ron Carter](/wiki/Ron_Carter "Ron Carter"), who played double bass on one track. [De La Soul](/wiki/De_La_Soul "De La Soul")'s *[Buhloone Mindstate](/wiki/Buhloone_Mindstate "Buhloone Mindstate")* ([Tommy Boy](/wiki/Tommy_Boy_records "Tommy Boy records"), 1993) featured contributions from [Maceo Parker](/wiki/Maceo_Parker "Maceo Parker"), [Fred Wesley](/wiki/Fred_Wesley "Fred Wesley"), and [Pee Wee Ellis](/wiki/Pee_Wee_Ellis "Pee Wee Ellis"), and samples from [Eddie Harris](/wiki/Eddie_Harris "Eddie Harris"), [Lou Donaldson](/wiki/Lou_Donaldson "Lou Donaldson"), [Duke Pearson](/wiki/Duke_Pearson "Duke Pearson") , and [Milt Jackson](/wiki/Milt_Jackson "Milt Jackson"). [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah") and [Monie Love](/wiki/Monie_Love "Monie Love") were members of [Native Tongues](/wiki/Native_Tongues "Native Tongues") , also.

Also of this period was Toronto-based [Dream Warriors](/wiki/Dream_Warriors_(band) "Dream Warriors (band)")' 1991 release *[And Now the Legacy Begins](/wiki/And_Now_the_Legacy_Begins "And Now the Legacy Begins")* ([Island](/wiki/Island_Records "Island Records")). It produced the hit singles "My Definition of a Boombastic Jazz Style" and "Wash Your Face in My Sink". The first of these was based on a loop taken from [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones")' "[Soul Bossa Nova](/wiki/Soul_Bossa_Nova "Soul Bossa Nova")", while the second sampled [Count Basie](/wiki/Count_Basie "Count Basie")'s 1967 rendition of "[Hang On Sloopy](/wiki/Hang_On_Sloopy "Hang On Sloopy")". Meanwhile, Los Angeles hip-hop group [Freestyle Fellowship](/wiki/Freestyle_Fellowship "Freestyle Fellowship") pursued a different route of jazz influence in recordings with unusual time signatures and [scat](/wiki/Scat_singing "Scat singing")-influenced vocals.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Jazz artists come to hip-hop.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_rap&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Jazz artists come to hip-hop.")]

Though jazz rap had achieved little mainstream success, jazz legend [Miles Davis](/wiki/Miles_Davis "Miles Davis")' final album (released posthumously in 1992), *[Doo-Bop](/wiki/Doo-Bop "Doo-Bop")*, featured hip-hop beats and collaborations with producer [Easy Mo Bee](/wiki/Easy_Mo_Bee "Easy Mo Bee").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Jazz musician [Branford Marsalis](/wiki/Branford_Marsalis "Branford Marsalis") collaborated with Gang Starr's [DJ Premier](/wiki/DJ_Premier "DJ Premier") on his [Buckshot LeFonque](/wiki/Buckshot_LeFonque "Buckshot LeFonque") project that same year. Between 1993 and 2007, fellow Gang Starr member [Guru](/wiki/Guru_(rapper) "Guru (rapper)") released the *[Jazzmatazz](/wiki/Jazzmatazz "Jazzmatazz")* series, which featured guest appearances from jazz artists such as [Lonnie Liston Smith](/wiki/Lonnie_Liston_Smith "Lonnie Liston Smith"), [Freddie Hubbard](/wiki/Freddie_Hubbard "Freddie Hubbard") , and [Donald Byrd](/wiki/Donald_Byrd "Donald Byrd"), amongst others.

### Since 1994

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jazz_rap&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Since 1994")]

Musical jazz references became less obvious and less sustained, and lyrical references to jazz certainly rarer.[[14]](#cite_note-14) However, jazz had been added to the palette of hip-hop producers, and its influence continued throughout the 1990s, whether behind the gritty street tales of [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas") (*[Illmatic](/wiki/Illmatic "Illmatic")*, [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Records "Columbia Records"), 1994), or backing the more bohemian sensibilities of acts such as [the Roots](/wiki/The_Roots "The Roots"), [the Nonce](/wiki/The_Nonce "The Nonce"), and [Common](/wiki/Common_(rapper) "Common (rapper)"). Since the 2000s, it can be detected in the work of producers such as [J. Rawls](/wiki/J._Rawls "J. Rawls"), [Fat Jon](/wiki/Fat_Jon "Fat Jon") , and [Madlib](/wiki/Madlib "Madlib"). A project somewhat similar to Buckshot Le Fonque was [Brooklyn Funk Essentials](/wiki/Brooklyn_Funk_Essentials "Brooklyn Funk Essentials"), a New York–based collective that also released its first LP in 1994. [Prince](/wiki/Prince_(musician) "Prince (musician)") himself contributed to the genre on some songs from 1991 to 1992, as well as with his [New Power Generation](/wiki/New_Power_Generation "New Power Generation") album *[Gold Nigga](/wiki/Gold_Nigga "Gold Nigga")*, which mixed jazz, funk, and hip-hop and was released very confidentially.

One hip hop project that continued to maintain a direct connection to jazz was [Guru](/wiki/Guru_(rapper) "Guru (rapper)")'s *[Jazzmatazz](/wiki/Jazzmatazz "Jazzmatazz")* series, which used live jazz musicians in the studio.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Spanning from 1993 to 2007, its four volumes assembled jazz luminaries like [Freddie Hubbard](/wiki/Freddie_Hubbard "Freddie Hubbard"), [Donald Byrd](/wiki/Donald_Byrd "Donald Byrd"), [Courtney Pine](/wiki/Courtney_Pine "Courtney Pine"), [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock"), [Kenny Garrett](/wiki/Kenny_Garrett "Kenny Garrett") , and [Lonnie Liston Smith](/wiki/Lonnie_Liston_Smith "Lonnie Liston Smith"), and hip-hop performers such as [Kool Keith](/wiki/Kool_Keith "Kool Keith"), [MC Solaar](/wiki/MC_Solaar "MC Solaar"), [Common](/wiki/Common_(rapper) "Common (rapper)"), and Guru's Gang Starr colleague [DJ Premier](/wiki/DJ_Premier "DJ Premier").

Madlib's 2003 release, *[Shades of Blue](/wiki/Shades_of_Blue_(Madlib_album) "Shades of Blue (Madlib album)")*, is said to pay homage to his [Blue Note Records](/wiki/Blue_Note_Records "Blue Note Records") roots, where he samples from Blue Note's archives. The album also contains interpretations of Blue Note classics, performed by Yesterdays New Quintet.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Kendrick Lamar's 2015 album *[To Pimp A Butterfly](/wiki/To_Pimp_A_Butterfly "To Pimp A Butterfly")* prominently featured jazz instrumentation on tracks, and its musical scope was critically acclaimed. Following the recording of the album, more recordings from the project with jazz, soul, and funk influences were released as the compilation album *[Untitled Unmastered](/wiki/Untitled_Unmastered "Untitled Unmastered")* in 2016.
